title: Kaposi sarcoma: human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8)
vignette: A 35-year-old man comes to the office due to a rash and weight loss. He uses intravenous heroin. Examination shows tender anterior and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. White plaques are present over the buccal mucosa and soft palate. Skin examination demonstrates a widespread rash consisting of lesions similar to that shown in the image below. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's skin findings?

clues.note: Intravenous drug use is a major risk factor for HIV (and for bloodborne hepatitis). Combined with weight loss, lymphadenopathy, and oral thrush, this points to an immunocompromised, likely HIV-positive host.

clues.text: tender anterior and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy
clues.note: Generalized lymphadenopathy plus constitutional weight loss is common in HIV infection. It frames the patient as immunosuppressed and prompts you to look for an AIDS-defining process to explain the skin lesions.

clues.text: White plaques are present over the buccal mucosa and soft palate
clues.note: White plaques on the buccal mucosa and palate are oral thrush (Candida). In an adult, oral candidiasis signals significant immunosuppression (eg, HIV/AIDS), strengthening the case that the skin lesions are an AIDS-associated tumor.

In an HIV-positive patient, widespread violaceous/red-brown papules and plaques are classic for Kaposi sarcoma, a vascular tumor and AIDS-defining malignancy. The image of such a lesion seals the diagnosis and points to its cause, HHV-8.

whatMatters: Strip the story to: an IV drug user with weight loss, cervical lymphadenopathy, and oral thrush - that is HIV/AIDS. The violaceous skin lesions are Kaposi sarcoma, an AIDS-defining vascular tumor caused by human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8). Answer D.
strategy: When a vignette stacks HIV risk factors (IV drug use) with immunosuppression markers (oral thrush, weight loss, lymphadenopathy) and violaceous skin lesions, think Kaposi sarcoma and recall its cause. This is a 'match the lesion to its virus' question: KS -> HHV-8; oral hairy leukoplakia -> EBV; molluscum -> poxvirus.

eliminations.label: EBV - lymphoma/oral hairy leukoplakia, not KS
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: Epstein-Barr virus causes infectious mononucleosis, oral hairy leukoplakia, and (in HIV) Burkitt and CNS lymphomas. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: EBV-related lymphoma can cause lymphadenopathy and weight loss, but not red-purple papular skin lesions; Kaposi sarcoma is caused by HHV-8. ✅ Correct if: the lesion were a lymphoma or a non-scrapable white plaque on the lateral tongue (oral hairy leukoplakia).

eliminations.label: hepatitis C - cryoglobulinemic purpura, not KS
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: hepatitis C (bloodborne, common in IV drug users) can cause cryoglobulinemia with palpable purpura. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: cryoglobulinemic purpura is a lower-extremity vasculitic rash with arthralgia, not the widespread violaceous plaques of KS; HCV does not cause Kaposi sarcoma. ✅ Correct if: palpable purpura + arthralgia + renal disease (cryoglobulinemia).

eliminations.label: HSV-1 - vesicles, not violaceous plaques
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: herpes simplex virus type 1 causes grouped oral/labial vesicles and herpetic whitlow. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: HSV lesions are clustered painful vesicles/ulcers, not the red-purple papules of Kaposi sarcoma. ✅ Correct if: clustered painful vesicles on the lip or finger.

eliminations.label: syphilis - copper rash on palms/soles, not KS
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: Treponema pallidum causes syphilis; secondary syphilis gives a diffuse maculopapular rash (including palms and soles) and condyloma lata. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the syphilitic rash is copper-colored and flat, not violaceous nodular KS; the thrush + IV drug use + weight loss point to HIV with Kaposi sarcoma. ✅ Correct if: a diffuse rash involving palms and soles with a painless chancre history.

reasoning: IV drug use + weight loss + cervical lymphadenopathy + oral thrush = HIV/AIDS
Widespread violaceous skin lesions in an AIDS patient = Kaposi sarcoma
Kaposi sarcoma is caused by human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8)
Cause of skin findings -> answer D
mechanism: Kaposi sarcoma is a vascular (endothelial) tumor caused by human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8) and is the most common malignancy in untreated HIV - an AIDS-defining illness. HHV-8 infects vascular and lymphatic endothelial cells and carries oncogenes that drive endothelial proliferation and angiogenesis. Cutaneous lesions are violaceous/red-brown macules that evolve into papules and plaques, often along skin folds; visceral spread (lungs, GI tract) can cause life-threatening bleeding. Histology shows spindle-shaped endothelial cells, slit-like vascular spaces, and extravasated red blood cells.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Micro/Path > HHV-8 and Kaposi sarcoma: endothelial tumor, HIV association, histology (spindle cells, slit-like vascular spaces). Cross-ref the HIV-associated conditions list (thrush, KS, lymphomas).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Two steps: (1) recognize HIV/AIDS from the risk factor + thrush + weight loss; (2) name the violaceous tumor (KS) and its virus (HHV-8). Don't stop at 'HIV' - the question asks the cause of the SKIN lesion.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on Kaposi sarcoma, HHV-8, oral thrush vs oral hairy leukoplakia, violaceous, cryoglobulinemia, condyloma lata? Tell me which term and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: EBV and syphilis tempt because both occur in HIV and cause systemic findings - but neither produces violaceous KS plaques. Match the specific lesion morphology to HHV-8.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: A clinical photo shows a violaceous/red-brown KS lesion. You don't strictly need it - the HIV context + lesion description suffice - but practice recognizing KS: dusky purple macules/papules/plaques, often on the lower extremities or oral mucosa.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Tell me what happened (timing, second-guessing, careless click) and I'll suggest a fix for future questions.

keywords.front: Violaceous/red-purple skin papules in an HIV patient

keywords.back: Spindle endothelial cells, slit-like vascular spaces, extravasated RBCs
Kaposi sarcoma, primary CNS lymphoma, invasive cervical cancer

firstAid.detail: Micro/Path > HHV-8: endothelial tumor in HIV; violaceous lesions; spindle cells, slit-like vascular spaces; visceral spread.
firstAid.location: Micro/Path > HHV-8: endothelial tumor in HIV; violaceous lesions; spindle cells, slit-like vascular spaces; visceral spread.

firstAid.detail: Micro > EBV (mono, oral hairy leukoplakia, lymphomas), HSV-1 (vesicles), HCV (cryoglobulinemia), Treponema pallidum (secondary syphilis rash on palms/soles).
firstAid.location: Micro > EBV (mono, oral hairy leukoplakia, lymphomas), HSV-1 (vesicles), HCV (cryoglobulinemia), Treponema pallidum (secondary syphilis rash on palms/soles).

firstAid.detail: Micro/Immuno > opportunistic infections and malignancies; oral candidiasis as a marker of immunosuppression.
firstAid.location: Micro/Immuno > opportunistic infections and malignancies; oral candidiasis as a marker of immunosuppression.

memoryHook: Kaposi = the 8th herpes (HHV-8). Violaceous plaques + thrush + IV drug use = HIV with KS.
